expanded_description: Learn to represent chemical reactions using word equations and skeleton equations, mastering the conventions for writing reactants, products, state symbols, and reaction conditions to accurately communicate chemical changes.

core_concept.principle: Chemical Equations Are the Language of Reactions
core_concept.explanation: Chemical equations provide a concise, standardized way to represent chemical reactions. Word equations use names of substances to show what reacts and what forms; skeleton equations use chemical formulas and symbols to represent the same reaction more precisely. Both follow the convention: reactants on the left, products on the right, separated by an arrow indicating the direction of change.

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison of word equation and skeleton equation for the same reaction
image.caption: Figure 7.2.1: Word equations use names; skeleton equations use formulas and symbols for precise chemical communication.

sections.introduction: Before learning the mechanics of equation writing, understand why chemists use this symbolic language: to communicate reactions clearly, predict products, and apply the Law of Conservation of Mass.

key_concept.definition: A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction showing the identities and relative amounts of reactants and products.
key_concept.context: Equations allow chemists worldwide to share reaction information unambiguously, regardless of spoken language.

principles.statement: A well-written equation tells you: what substances react, what substances form, their physical states, and any special conditions required.
principles.application: $\text{2H}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \xrightarrow{\text{spark}} \text{2H}_2\text{O}(l)$ communicates reactants, products, states, and ignition requirement in one line.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe: ingredients (reactants) + instructions (conditions) → finished dish (products).

principles.statement: Every equation must represent an actual chemical change that conserves mass and charge; you cannot invent reactions that violate fundamental laws.
principles.application: You can write $\text{Fe} + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS}$ (real reaction), but not $\text{Fe} + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS}_2$ without evidence that pyrite forms under those conditions.
principles.example: Always verify reactions with experimental data or reliable references before writing equations.

classifications.behavior: Equations serve as both descriptive records and predictive tools in chemical problem-solving.

examples.explanation: Writing $\text{Zn} + \text{2HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ records exactly what occurred, allowing others to replicate the experiment.
The equation $\text{N}_2 + \text{3H}_2 \rightarrow \text{2NH}_3$ allows engineers to calculate exact reactant amounts needed to produce tons of ammonia.

sections.introduction: Word equations are the simplest way to represent a chemical reaction, using the names of reactants and products connected by an arrow.

key_concept.definition: A word equation writes the names of reactants on the left, an arrow (→) meaning 'yields' or 'produces', and the names of products on the right.
key_concept.context: Word equations are useful for initial reaction description before learning chemical formulas; they focus on substance identities rather than quantities.

components.interpretation: The '+' sign means 'reacts with' (left side) or 'and' (right side); the arrow '→' means 'yields' or 'produces'.

image.alt: Labeled diagram of word equation components
image.caption: Figure 7.2.2: Word equation anatomy: reactants, arrow, products, and connecting symbols.

categories.description: List all reactants separated by '+', followed by '→', then all products separated by '+'. Use correct chemical names.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Identify substances before and after the reaction from observations or descriptions; write their standard chemical names; arrange in reactant → product order.

examples.explanation: Observation: shiny metal + oxygen gas → white powder. Word equation: magnesium + oxygen → magnesium oxide

image.caption: Word equation captures the substance change.

examples.explanation: Observation: liquid breaks down into water and bubbles of gas. Word equation: hydrogen peroxide → water + oxygen

image.caption: Single reactant can yield multiple products.

categories.description: Add conditions above or below the arrow when they are essential for the reaction to occur.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Common conditions: heat (Δ), light (hν), catalyst, electricity, specific temperature/pressure. Place condition text over the arrow.

examples.explanation: Water doesn't decompose spontaneously; electricity is required. Word equation: water $\xrightarrow{\text{electricity}}$ hydrogen + oxygen
Requires light energy and chlorophyll. Word equation: carbon dioxide + water $\xrightarrow{\text{light, chlorophyll}}$ glucose + oxygen

categories.description: Use standard IUPAC or common names consistently; avoid ambiguous terms.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Prefer 'sodium chloride' over 'salt'; 'hydrogen gas' or 'dihydrogen' for $\text{H}_2$; specify oxidation states when needed (iron(II) vs iron(III)).

examples.explanation: Use 'iron(III) oxide' not just 'rust' for precision. Word equation: iron + oxygen → iron(III) oxide
Distinguish copper(I) oxide ($\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, red) from copper(II) oxide ($\text{CuO}$, black) in equations.

worked_examples.title: Writing a Word Equation from Observation
worked_examples.problem: When solid calcium carbonate is heated, it breaks down into solid calcium oxide and carbon dioxide gas. Write the word equation.

step_by_step.explanation: Only one substance starts the reaction: calcium carbonate (solid).
Two substances form: calcium oxide (solid) and carbon dioxide (gas).
The reaction requires heating; add 'heat' or Δ above the arrow.
Place reactant on left, products on right, arrow with condition in middle.
step_by_step.calculation: calcium carbonate $\xrightarrow{\text{heat}}$ calcium oxide + carbon dioxide

worked_examples.final_answer: calcium carbonate $\xrightarrow{\text{heat}}$ calcium oxide + carbon dioxide

worked_examples.title: Word Equation with Multiple Reactants
worked_examples.problem: Aqueous silver nitrate reacts with aqueous sodium chloride to produce solid silver chloride and aqueous sodium nitrate. Write the word equation.

step_by_step.explanation: Two starting materials: silver nitrate (aqueous) and sodium chloride (aqueous).
Two products form: silver chloride (solid precipitate) and sodium nitrate (aqueous).
Reaction occurs at room temperature when solutions mix; no special condition needed.
Combine with proper formatting; states can be noted in parentheses if desired.
step_by_step.calculation: silver nitrate(aq) + sodium chloride(aq) → silver chloride(s) + sodium nitrate(aq)

worked_examples.final_answer: silver nitrate(aq) + sodium chloride(aq) → silver chloride(s) + sodium nitrate(aq)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multi-Reactant Word Equation with State Symbols

sections.introduction: Skeleton equations replace substance names with chemical formulas, adding state symbols and reaction arrows for precise, quantitative representation.

key_concept.definition: A skeleton equation uses chemical formulas for reactants and products, includes state symbols (s, l, g, aq), and shows reaction direction with '→', but is not yet balanced for mass conservation.
key_concept.context: Skeleton equations are the intermediate step between word equations and fully balanced chemical equations; they focus on correct formulas before stoichiometry.

characteristics: Use standard symbols from periodic table
Apply correct subscripts from ion charges or bonding rules
Write elements as they exist naturally (e.g., $\text{O}_2$, not O)
Polyatomic ions stay together in formulas
classifications.behavior: Incorrect formulas make equations meaningless; always verify formulas before writing equations.

examples.explanation: Seven elements exist as diatomic molecules: $\text{H}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{F}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$, $\text{I}_2$. Write $\text{O}_2$, not O, for oxygen gas.
Balance charges: $\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ combine as $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, not AlO. Use criss-cross method or lowest common multiple of charges.

classifications.behavior: State symbols provide critical context: reactions often depend on physical state; precipitates form only when (aq) reactants produce (s) products.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$: the (s) on AgCl indicates the insoluble product that forms the precipitate.

image.caption: State symbol (s) identifies the solid product.

examples.explanation: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + \text{2HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$: the (g) on $\text{CO}_2$ explains the observed bubbles.

image.caption: State symbol (g) identifies the gaseous product.

characteristics: catalyst name above arrow : substance that speeds reaction
classifications.behavior: These symbols communicate reaction conditions and reversibility; omitting them can misrepresent the chemistry.

examples.explanation: $\text{N}_2(g) + \text{3H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{2NH}_3(g)$: double arrow indicates ammonia formation and decomposition occur simultaneously at equilibrium.
$\text{2H}_2\text{O}_2(aq) \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2} \text{2H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{O}_2(g)$: manganese(IV) oxide speeds decomposition but is not consumed.

principles.statement: A skeleton equation shows correct formulas and states but may not have equal atoms on both sides; balancing adjusts coefficients (not subscripts) to satisfy conservation of mass.
principles.analogy: Skeleton equation is like a recipe with ingredients listed but wrong amounts; balancing fixes the quantities.

principles.statement: Subscripts define chemical identity; changing $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ to $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ changes water to hydrogen peroxide—a different substance.
principles.application: To balance oxygen in $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$, add coefficient: $\text{2H}_2\text{O}$, not change subscript to $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$.
principles.example: Remember: coefficients multiply entire formulas; subscripts are part of the formula itself.

worked_examples.title: Converting Word Equation to Skeleton Equation
worked_examples.problem: Convert this word equation to a skeleton equation: magnesium + hydrochloric acid → magnesium chloride + hydrogen

step_by_step.explanation: magnesium = $\text{Mg}(s)$ (metal); hydrochloric acid = $\text{HCl}(aq)$; magnesium chloride = $\text{MgCl}_2(aq)$ (Mg²⁺ needs two Cl⁻); hydrogen = $\text{H}_2(g)$ (diatomic).
Mg is solid metal; HCl is aqueous acid; MgCl₂ is soluble salt (aq); H₂ is gas.
Place reactants left, products right, arrow between. Do not balance yet.

worked_examples.title: Writing Skeleton Equation with Polyatomic Ions
worked_examples.problem: Write the skeleton equation: aqueous sodium phosphate reacts with aqueous calcium chloride to produce solid calcium phosphate and aqueous sodium chloride.

step_by_step.explanation: Sodium phosphate: $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ → $\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4$; Calcium chloride: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ → $\text{CaCl}_2$; Calcium phosphate: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ → $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$; Sodium chloride: $\text{NaCl}$.
All reactants aqueous (soluble salts); calcium phosphate is insoluble (s); sodium chloride is soluble (aq).
Combine formulas and states; keep polyatomic ions in parentheses when subscripted.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Polyatomic Ion Handling in Skeleton Equations

sections.introduction: Apply your skills to translate real-world chemical descriptions into accurate word and skeleton equations.

key_concept.definition: The process: (1) Identify reactants and products from description, (2) Write correct names/formulas, (3) Add states and conditions, (4) Format with proper symbols.
key_concept.context: This workflow is used in lab reports, research papers, and industrial documentation to communicate chemical changes precisely.

worked_examples.title: Combustion Reaction
worked_examples.problem: Methane gas burns in oxygen gas to produce carbon dioxide gas and water vapor. Write both word and skeleton equations.

step_by_step.explanation: List names: methane + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water. Add states in parentheses if desired.
Methane = $\text{CH}_4$; oxygen = $\text{O}_2$ (diatomic); carbon dioxide = $\text{CO}_2$; water = $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
All gases in this combustion; use → for irreversible reaction.

worked_examples.final_answer:
Word: methane + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water
Skeleton: $\text{CH}_4(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ (unbalanced)

worked_examples.title: Decomposition with Conditions
worked_examples.problem: Solid potassium chlorate decomposes when heated with a manganese(IV) oxide catalyst to produce solid potassium chloride and oxygen gas. Write the skeleton equation.

step_by_step.explanation: Potassium chlorate: $\text{KClO}_3$ (K⁺ + ClO₃⁻); potassium chloride: $\text{KCl}$; oxygen: $\text{O}_2(g)$. Catalyst $\text{MnO}_2$ is not a reactant.
KClO₃(s), KCl(s), O₂(g). Catalyst written above arrow.
Heat (Δ) and catalyst ($\text{MnO}_2$) go above the arrow.
Combine all elements in proper format.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Including Conditions and Catalysts in Equations

worked_examples.title: Double Replacement with Precipitate
worked_examples.problem: Aqueous lead(II) nitrate reacts with aqueous potassium iodide to form solid lead(II) iodide and aqueous potassium nitrate. Write the skeleton equation.

step_by_step.explanation: Lead(II) nitrate: $\text{Pb}^{2+}$ + $\text{NO}_3^-$ → $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2$; Potassium iodide: $\text{K}^+$ + $\text{I}^-$ → $\text{KI}$; Lead(II) iodide: $\text{PbI}_2$; Potassium nitrate: $\text{KNO}_3$.
Nitrates and Group 1 salts are soluble (aq); lead iodide is insoluble (s) — this is the precipitate.
Reactants left, products right, arrow between. Keep polyatomic ions in parentheses.

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when writing equations; apply expert strategies for accuracy and efficiency.

key_concept.definition: Equation writing requires attention to detail: correct formulas, proper states, appropriate symbols, and awareness of common pitfalls.
key_concept.context: Even experienced chemists double-check equations; developing good habits early prevents persistent errors.

classifications.value: Always Write H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂
characteristics: Writing O instead of O₂ for oxygen gas
Using Cl instead of Cl₂ in reactions
classifications.behavior: Incorrect elemental formulas invalidate the entire equation; the reaction may appear unbalanced or impossible.

characteristics: Changing H₂O to H₂O₂ to 'balance' oxygen
Altering compound formulas to make atoms match
classifications.behavior: Changing subscripts changes the chemical identity; you're no longer representing the intended reaction.

examples.explanation: Wrong: $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ (makes hydrogen peroxide); Correct: $\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O}$ (balances with coefficients).

image.caption: Coefficients go in front; subscripts stay in formula.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use a Formula Reference
classifications.value: Keep a List of Common Ions and Formulas Handy
characteristics: Know solubility rules to assign (aq) vs (s)
Reference periodic table for elemental forms
classifications.behavior: Having reliable references reduces errors and builds confidence in equation writing.

examples.explanation: Knowing $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ has -3 charge lets you quickly write $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$ for calcium phosphate without guessing.

image.caption: Reference charts speed accurate formula writing.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Verify with Conservation Check
classifications.value: Even for Skeleton Equations, Do a Quick Atom Count
characteristics: Count atoms of each element on both sides
Prepare mentally for the balancing step
classifications.behavior: Early awareness of imbalances makes the balancing step (Section 7.4) faster and more intuitive.

examples.explanation: For $\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$: C:1=1✓, H:4≠2✗, O:2≠3✗ → know H and O need balancing.

image.caption: Quick counts reveal what needs balancing.

principles.statement: Follow this sequence for reliable results: (1) Identify substances, (2) Write correct formulas, (3) Add states, (4) Format with symbols, (5) Verify formulas, (6) Balance (later).
principles.application: This systematic approach prevents skipping critical steps and reduces errors in complex reactions.
principles.analogy: Like building a house: foundation (formulas) must be correct before adding walls (states) and roof (balancing).

summary: Chemical equations communicate reactions using standardized symbolic language. Word equations use substance names (reactants → products) for conceptual clarity; skeleton equations replace names with chemical formulas, add state symbols (s, l, g, aq), and include reaction conditions above the arrow for precision. Key conventions: write diatomic elements as H₂, N₂, O₂, etc.; keep polyatomic ions intact in formulas; never change subscripts to balance—use coefficients instead. The workflow: (1) identify reactants/products from description, (2) write correct formulas using ion charges and bonding rules, (3) assign physical states based on solubility and conditions, (4) format with proper symbols and arrows. Skeleton equations are intentionally unbalanced; balancing coefficients to satisfy conservation of mass is the next step (Section 7.4). Mastering equation writing enables accurate documentation, prediction, and quantitative analysis of chemical reactions.
